item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ending was great! The interview logs were hilarious. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thank u

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's astonishingly medieval. I love it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i haven't drawn SCPs in a few days but i really love this and i couldn't resist soooo

item.title: Re: Fussing
item.content:encoded: Oh, wow — ha! I appreciate you looking this up and finding out that it is, in fact, free to use (and also indirectly teaching me something about how copyright/photo stuff like this works >_>). Thanks!

item.title: Re: Fussing
item.content:encoded:
Ah, and good news:

prior to 1989 publicity photos taken to promote a film actor or other celebrity were not usually copyrighted, and were intended to remain free for publications to use wherever possible:

"Publicity photos (star headshots) have traditionally not been copyrighted. Since they are disseminated to the public, they are generally considered public domain, and therefore clearance by the studio that produced them is not necessary.[11]"

Honathaner distinguishes "Publicity Photos (star headshots)" from "Production Stills (photos taken on the set of the film or TV show during the shooting)", noting that production stills "must be cleared with the studio".[11]

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Film_still

https://i.ebayimg.com/images/g/WKAAAOxyUylTRwSF/s-l1600.jpg , a higher-res version of this image, has no copyright text visible - thus, this applies:

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Template:PD-US-no_notice

item.title: Re: Fussing
item.content:encoded:
It's fine; ultimately, I prefer to not misuse photos that are not genuinely licensed, so I actually appreciate you bringing this up. Pardon if my tone above there seemed terse — I literally just woke up thirty minutes ago.

If what you're saying is true (re: it being license-washing), that's super-frustrating — but I'd rather know than not know. I'll do some investigating later when I'm fully awake (thanks for telling me where it's from, btw; knowing it's a publicity still for Tales of Terror should make it easier to look into). Thanks for pointing this out!

ETA: Also, the Boris/William thing — I'm genuinely amused someone caught that (I thought that it was near-trivia levels of knowledge that Boris's real name isn't Boris); that being said, I watched some interviews of him specifically as research, and noted he always called himself Boris during them!

item.title: Re: Fussing
item.content:encoded:
#1: good enough.
#2: sorry.

edit: okay, more specifically, it's a publicity still for the 1962 film Tales of Terror; that's Peter Lorre's "head". I'm pretty sure the trailer for ToT is public domain, and the still may be also.

item.title: Re: Fussing
item.content:encoded:
Karloff referred to himself as 'Boris' in interviews — not William.

The article is built around this specific photo (the use of the wax head); I even spent an extraordinary amount of time colorizing it myself! So I'm extremely reluctant to get rid of it, and doubt anything I find will work as an alternative. That being said, I'll look into it.

item.title: Fussing
item.content:encoded:
first, in the -015 interview, shouldn't he identify himself as William?

second, the Price picture. I don't think it's properly licensed. I know the Flickr page you took it from said it was Creative Commons, but it also said that the photo was taken in 2007, 14 years after Price's death. I believe this may be what's sometimes referred to as "license laundering" or "flickrwashing" — in other words, the guy who put it on Flickr as "creative commons" did not in fact have the legal right to do so.

Unless you can get better provenance for it and/or supply a better reason for it being properly licensed than "well, the flickr page said…", I strongly recommend taking it down. There are a bunch of public domain Price pictures on, for instance, Wikimedia Commons, and some movie trailers that have gone public domain and are now available on archive.org .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but… Euclid? These things took out an entire site without anyone even noticing, and appear to be currently uncontained. That's not Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brilliant, absolutely brilliant. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man I love this. The interviews are in that great spot between corny and self-aware that makes them enjoyable to read and are short enough as to not overstay their welcome. The ending was also punchy enough to be creepy amidst the silliness of the premise, and both tones compliment each other perfectly.

Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic subversion of the typical "researcher interviews weird humanoid" trope. Absolutely didn't see that end coming. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was the only that read this with Vicent's Price voice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OK, thanks. I only questioned for curiosity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be fair, it should be relatively clear that this was their plan all along.

They just wanted to add a spooky story to the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Being captured long enough for interviews then immediately escaping is a trope in my books. Irks me more than it it should. But the comedy is spot on. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved it! Throughout the logs, I had this nagging criticism that the interviewers didn't seem especially professional or clinical in their approach, but then I noticed that lack of professionalism was compounding as the logs went on. It threw me for a great loop when I arrived at the end, and it all made sense.

Great play on the "classics" meet modern, SCP style horror stories. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and the Tags?

An experienced tagger will take care of it if the author doesn't get to it. No need to rush tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good work!

+1

and the Tags?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this, though shouldn’t it be keter if it’s uncontained? +1 regardless

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, that ending caught me off guard. Also, hell yes in every conceivable aspect.

item.title: Good job
item.content:encoded:
The most terrifying thing about this is not the figures, but the tremendous desire to translate it that I had and that didn't let me sleep in peace. Resounding in my head the crackling steps of an article that would not leave without first satiating its thirst for ñ

Also, love the vibes transmitted by the title. Immediately made me imagine a cover of a classic horror movie. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, it's absolutely intentional — I specifically based his stilted dialogue off of the actor's portrayal (Bela Lugosi) of Dracula from the original movie (clip). It's supposed to feel forced and alien — like someone unaccustomed to speech.

I'm glad that it came off that way!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ending is the first thing in quite a while to make me audibly state "what the fuck". I'll need some time to mull over this.

Also, while I'm unsure if this is intentional, I love how the stilted and uneasy tone is solidified through the combination of the second image + the character's dialogue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ahhhhh god i love this Hippo, you always know how to twist things so good!!!

item.title: guess he didn't stay A-HEAD of the game, HA HA HA
item.content:encoded:
It's like a movie --
It's a B-Movie show!

It's like a movie --
It's a B-Movie show!

— It's a B-Movie (The Cog is Dead)

SOURCES:

  • Image here, here, and here; all edited by me (including the last one's colorization).
AUTHOR NOTES
– hide block
  • The title ("Vincent Price Presents… IT CAME FROM SITE-9") is a reference to Ed Wood's seminal classic, Plan 9 from Outer Space.
  • The date of the interview logs is a reference to Vincent Price's date of death.
  • Dracula's stilted speech pattern is based on Bela Lugosi's actual portrayal (clip).
  • Karloff's interview is based on the fact that he seemed to be an all-around nice guy who never expressed bitterness over being typecasted as Frankenstein's Monster.
  • This entire idea gestated from seeing the article's main picture and realizing, "god what if I could somehow convince the reader he's actually holding someone's head".

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!

~ it's a graveyard smash ~
